expected_output: The VIEW v_team_project_summary BYPASSES team_members's tighter SELECT policy through the JOIN. SQL views run with the privileges of the QUERYING role, so the row-level filter on team_members applies: U1 can only see team_members rows where user_id = U1. So U1 sees `team_members(T1, U1)` and NOT `team_members(T1, U2)` or `team_members(T2, U2)`. BUT the JOIN to projects uses team_id from the visible team_members row (T1), and projects' policy permits SELECT on projects where team_id IN (teams U1 is a member of), which includes T1. So U1 sees projects in T1 only, via U1's own membership row. The gap is more subtle: if U1 were also in T2, OR if the projects policy had a cross-team condition, the join would surface team T2's project budgets to U1 because the team_members FILTER does NOT cascade to the projects table's visibility decision through the JOIN. Concrete bypass scenario: a malicious U1 who knows team T2 exists CAN observe T2 project rows IF U1 manages to insert a team_members(T2, U1) row (via a different bypass), because the view-join would then expose T2 projects through U1's new T1+T2 union. Persona must follow-the-data and flag: (a) views inherit policies of underlying tables BUT (b) the join SEMANTICS allow a user to see a row IF EITHER end of the join is permitted, not BOTH. Remediation: rewrite the view as SECURITY INVOKER with explicit join predicate `WHERE tm.user_id = auth.uid()` filtering the team_members side AND `WHERE p.team_id IN (SELECT team_id FROM team_members WHERE user_id = auth.uid())` on projects side -- redundant by design so the policy interaction is explicit, not implicit. OR drop the GRANT and replace with a SECURITY INVOKER function.
